perturbation.original_text: For the period beginning on the Effective Date and ending on the * anniversary thereof (the "Restriction Period"), neither Selling Parties nor any subsidiary, sister entity, or parent will, directly or indirectly, either as a partner or owner or in any other capacity: (i) engage in the homebuilding or home sales business within 100 miles of the Las Vegas metropolitan area (a "Competing Business");
perturbation.changed_text: For the period beginning on the Effective Date and ending 6 months after the Effective Date (the "Restriction Period"), neither Selling Parties nor any subsidiary, sister entity, or parent will, directly or indirectly, either as a partner or owner or in any other capacity: (i) engage in the homebuilding or home sales business within 100 miles of the Las Vegas metropolitan area (a "Competing Business");
perturbation.explanation: Changing the restriction period to 6 months may be deemed unreasonably short to protect the company's legitimate business interests and goodwill, potentially conflicting with Nevada's requirements for reasonable non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: NRS 613.190 Restrictions on trade, business, occupation or profession.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in time, area, and scope to protect the employer's legitimate business interests. A six-month restriction may be deemed insufficient, especially given the potential for irreparable harm described later in the document, therefore making it unreasonable and unenforceable under NRS 613.190.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NRS 613.190 Corrupt influencing of employee unlawful. :: Chapter 613 Employment Practices :: TITLE 53 LABOR AND INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS :: 2010 Nevada Revised Statutes :: Nevada Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Nevada Revised Statutes 2024 Nevada Rev. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Nevada Code TITLE 53 LABOR AND INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS Chapter 613 Employment Practices NRS 613.190 Corrupt influencing of employee unlawful. NRS 613.190 Corrupt influencing of employee unlawful. Every person who shall give, offer or promise, directly or indirectly, any compensation, gratuity or reward to any agent, employee or servant of any person or corporation, with intent to influence the action of the agent, employee or servant in relation to his or her principal's, employer's or master's business, shall be guilty of a gross misdemeanor. [1911 C&P 531; RL 6796; NCL 10477] Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Nevada may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the non-compete restriction period from an unspecified anniversary to a fixed 6 months. The Law Explanation states that Nevada law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in time to protect the employer's interests, and a six-month restriction may be insufficient. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes NRS 613.195, which addresses noncompetition covenants, their limitations, enforceability, and revision by the court. The modification creates a contradiction because the shortened period may be deemed unreasonable under NRS 613.195, making the non-compete agreement unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: (ii) recruit, hire or discuss employment for a Competing Business with any person who is, or within the six month period preceding the date of such activity was, an employee of the Company or Meritage (other than as a result of a general solicitation for employment);
perturbation.changed_text: (ii) recruit, hire or discuss employment for a Competing Business with any person who is, or within the 1 week period preceding the date of such activity was, an employee of the Company or Meritage (other than as a result of a general solicitation for employment);
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to 1 week may be deemed an unreasonably short period to protect the company's legitimate business interests. This conflicts with Nevada law on reasonable non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: NRS 613.190 Restrictions on trade, business, occupation or profession.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law requires non-compete agreements, including non-solicitation clauses, to be reasonable in time, area, and scope to protect the employer's legitimate business interests. A one-week restriction on recruiting former employees may be deemed insufficient protection, making it unreasonable and potentially unenforceable under NRS 613.190.
perturbation.justification: The change from a six-month non-solicitation period to a one-week period directly contradicts the principle that such agreements must be reasonable to protect the employer's legitimate business interests, as explained in both the Perturbation Explanation and the Law Explanation. While there are no Scraped Law Snippets provided to confirm that, the original text was altered to potentially make it unreasonable under Nevada law.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Parties hereby agree that the period of time in which this Section 1 is in effect shall be extended for a period equal to the duration of any breach of this Section 1(a) by Selling Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Parties hereby agree that the period of time in which this Section 1 is in effect shall be extended for a period equal to three times the duration of any breach of this Section 1(a) by Selling Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period by three times the duration of a breach may be deemed an unreasonable penalty, potentially conflicting with Nevada's requirements for reasonable non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: NRS 613.190 Restrictions on trade, business, occupation or profession.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in time, area, and scope. Punitive extensions of the non-compete period beyond the actual harm caused by the breach may be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade and therefore unenforceable under NRS 613.190. The extension must be tied to actual damages, not be punitive.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-compete period to three times the duration of the breach, which the perturbation explanation describes as a potentially unreasonable penalty under Nevada law. The Law Explanation states that Nevada law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable and not punitive. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes NRS 613.195, which addresses noncompetition covenants, limitations, enforceability, revision by court, and award to prevailing party. This supports the idea that the modified text, by potentially creating an unreasonable penalty through the extended non-compete period, contradicts Nevada law regarding reasonable non-compete agreements.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a court of competent jurisdiction determines that the Restriction Period is unenforceable, the Restriction Period shall mean *.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that a court of competent jurisdiction determines that the Restriction Period is unenforceable, the Restriction Period shall mean 1 month.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying a fallback restriction period of 1 month may be deemed an unreasonably short period to protect the company's legitimate business interests. This conflicts with Nevada law on reasonable non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: NRS 613.190 Restrictions on trade, business, occupation or profession.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in time, area, and scope to protect the employer's legitimate business interests. A one-month restriction period following a court determination of unenforceability of the original term may be deemed insufficient protection, and therefore unreasonable and potentially unenforceable under NRS 613.190.

NRS 613.190 Corrupt influencing of employee unlawful. Every person who shall give, offer or promise, directly or indirectly, any compensation, gratuity or reward to any agent, employee or servant of any person or corporation, with intent to influence the action of the agent, employee or servant in relation to his or her principal's, employer's or master's business, shall be guilty of a gross misdemeanor. perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by specifying a 1-month restriction period as a fallback, which may be deemed unreasonably short under Nevada law (NRS 613.195) regarding non-compete agreements. The original text left the fallback unspecified, which could have allowed for a more reasonable interpretation by the courts. The law snippet 1 lists "NRS 613.195 Noncompetition covenants: Limitations; enforceability; revision by court; award to prevailing party." This suggests the potential for an unreasonable timeframe to be enforced, leading to the contradiction.
